note: Growing reference library for the NYC Modernization strategy. Append entries here; references.html renders it and build-references.py regenerates REFERENCES.md.

categories.name: The initiative — NYC Public Interest Technology (PIT) Crew
categories.blurb: The city program this project is written to inform and equip.

refs.title: NYC PIT Crew
refs.note: “We’re software engineers, user researchers, designers, and product managers building digital public goods for New Yorkers, in-house—to make interacting with government dignified and delightful for every New Yorker.”

refs.title: Mayor Mamdani Launches “Public Interest Technology (PIT) Crew” to Rapidly Build Digital Solutions to Public Problems
refs.note: Five PIT Crews deployed with OTI; first crew builds a DCWP “Click to Cancel” complaint portal; “from idea to implementation in a matter of months” instead of years.

categories.name: API Evangelist — the argument, over the years
categories.blurb: Kin Lane has made this case since 2011. The NYC assessment is the evidence.

refs.title: Every City, County, and State Should Have an API
refs.note: The problem isn’t bureaucracy, it’s the vendor tooling (Tyler/Munis). “PDF is not Portable Data Format… we need to expect more out of the technology purchased with our tax dollars.”

refs.title: APIs Can Save Money and Make Government More Efficient
refs.note: The US Department of Energy estimated it saved $882,000 by bundling an API with its bioenergy study.

refs.title: The Next Iteration of Government Data
refs.note: Do it “agency by agency, project by project,” with an open, collaborative, forkable process on GitHub.

refs.title: A Huge Need for Writeable APIs in Government
refs.note: “There isn’t much in the wild… huge demand… this could be the thing that changes how government operates.” The write-layer gap, called in 2013 — measured across 67 agencies in 2026.

refs.title: Formalizing My Approach to Identifying the Low-Hanging API Fruit
refs.note: The outside-in assessment method this project runs, agency by agency.

categories.name: Prior landscape analyses (the method, applied)
categories.blurb: The same method, run before at other institutions.

refs.title: An Outside-In Approach to Jumpstarting an API Effort at the University of Oklahoma
Department of Veterans Affairs API Landscape Analysis
refs.note: A federal-agency application of the same landscape method.

categories.name: Standards & tooling this project publishes
categories.blurb: The open, forkable substrate NYC can lead with.

refs.title: nyc-commons — the shared schema set
refs.note: One canonical borough / BBL / address / place / party / dollar for all of NYC government.

refs.title: The Programmable City — APIs · MCP · Skills
refs.note: The REST → MCP → Agent-Skill chain across 67 agencies, with a callable reference API and installable MCP.

refs.title: APIs.json specification
refs.note: The discovery format for cataloging the civic API surface.

refs.title: Open311 (GeoReport v2)
refs.note: The open standard for 311-style service requests — the write layer NYC pioneered and let lapse.

refs.title: Model Context Protocol
refs.note: The open standard making city services agent-native.

categories.name: Context & commentary
categories.blurb: Outside analysis that frames why this work matters.

refs.title: Mamdani invests in tech capacity to “solve real problems”
refs.source: Don Moynihan & Pamela Herd · Can We Still Govern?
refs.note: The scholars who named “administrative burden” read PIT Crew as delivering on housing, childcare, and consumer/worker protection by improving the experience of government — “every dead-end portal” erodes trust. The lens behind the project's research notes.

categories.name: Data & reports — everyday problems
categories.blurb: Outside diagnoses of New Yorkers' conditions that this project's data can help act on.

refs.title: Caucus 2025: Important Information for New Yorkers (report library)
refs.note: ~40 reports diagnosing everyday problems — chronic absenteeism, child poverty, child care, housing/homelessness, food insecurity, overdose, domestic violence, mental health, traffic deaths, hate crime, elder care, broadband, cost of living. Mapped to NYC agencies, data, and ideas in research.md.
